content.problemStatement:
Let SSS be a string of length NNN, containing only the characters `'L'` and `'R'`, with the following properties:


N≥3N \ge 3N≥3
S1=S2=RS_1 = S_2 = \text{R}S1​=S2​=R
SN=LS_N = \text{L}SN​=L
There is also a strip of NNN cells, numbered 111 to NNN from left to right.

Suppose you are standing at position XXX on the strip. Then,


If SX=RS_X = \text{R}SX​=R, you will move one step right.
If SX=LS_X = \text{L}SX​=L, you will move two steps left.
It can be proved that with the initial conditions imposed on the string SSS, your position will always lie in [1,N][1, N][1,N].

You don't know the string SSS.

Instead, you are given NNN and an array AAA of length NNN, which represents the following:


For each iii (1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N), if you start at position iii and follow the movement rules **exactly** NNN times, you will end up at position AiA_iAi​.
Determine if there exists a string SSS of length NNN such that S1=S2=RS_1 = S_2 = \text{R}S1​=S2​=R and SN=LS_N = \text{L}SN​=L such that moving according to it will result in the given array AAA.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: `Yes` if a valid string SSS exists, and `No` otherwise.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e. the strings `NO`, `No`, `nO`, and `no` will be considered equivalent.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 3⋅1053\cdot 10^53⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The string S=RRLS = \text{RRL}S=RRL will result in A=[1,2,3]A = [1, 2, 3]A=[1,2,3], because:

Starting at i=1i = 1i=1 and following the movement N=3N =3N=3 times, we'll move right once, right once again, and then left two steps, ending back at 111.
Starting at i=2i = 2i=2 and following the movement N=3N=3N=3 times, we'll move right once, left two steps, and right once again, ending at 222.
Starting at i=3i = 3i=3 and following the movement N=3N=3N=3 times, we'll move left two steps, right once, and right once again, ending at 333.
So, A=[1,2,3]A = [1, 2, 3]A=[1,2,3], as desired.

Test case 222: It can be proved that no string of length 333 results in A=[3,2,1]A = [3, 2, 1]A=[3,2,1].
